summary: Spolsky reviews Michael Porter's Competitive Strategy and applies its framework to Microsoft's antitrust behavior. He argues that Microsoft's refusal to appear contrite about antitrust violations is a deliberate strategic choice, not delusion. By maintaining an aggressive posture, Microsoft preserves the 'fear factor' that discourages VCs and startups from entering its markets. Spolsky draws a parallel to IBM's decline, where consent decree oversight made IBM ineffectual against competitors like young Microsoft. The post demonstrates how academic business strategy frameworks illuminate real-world tech industry dynamics.
key_points: Porter's framework suggests analyzing how regulatory constraints like antitrust affect a competitor's willingness to react to smaller rivals
Microsoft's refusal to concede any wrongdoing in its antitrust case is a calculated strategy to maintain fear among potential competitors and VCs
If Microsoft showed any contrition, it would signal to startups and investors that they could safely enter Microsoft's markets without being crushed
Microsoft learned from IBM's example: consent decree oversight paralyzed IBM and allowed smaller companies to compete against it effectively
The Netscape case served as a deliberate warning to VCs like Kleiner Perkins to stay out of Microsoft's territory
money_quotes: Lemming VCs generally have a policy of automatically saying 'no' to any investment that could be seen as competing with Microsoft or which might prod Microsoft to create a new product in response.
This herd fear of Microsoft in the software industry is enormously beneficial to Bill … lots of potential competition never gets off the ground because of fear that Microsoft will squash it.

If Microsoft were to act contrite and concede even the slightest point in the Justice Department's anti-trust case, hundreds of independent software companies and VCs would see this as their opportunity to enter Microsoft's markets.

Everything IBM did had to be reviewed by internal consent decree committees to make sure it wasn't monopolistic, and it was no problem for young companies (like, um, Microsoft) to take big bites out of IBM's ankles without provoking a reaction.
Microsoft's squashing of Netscape could well have been intended as a stern warning across the bow of Kleiner Perkins to stay the hell out of their garden.
It's a brilliant book and it is considered the standard text on the subject, read by freshly hatched management consultants worldwide. But it's drier than school cafeteria chicken.
Every sentence makes me look up at the ceiling, say 'Ah-ha!' and rethink some aspect of how the software industry works in a new light.
key_insight: Microsoft's seemingly delusional refusal to acknowledge antitrust wrongdoing is actually a rational strategy to preserve the fear that keeps competitors and investors from challenging its dominance.
